findings.title: Invalid predicted_direction silently flips the statistical tail (supported↔contradicted)
findings.description: Hypothesis.predicted_direction is typed Direction = Literal['greater','less'] but is NEVER validated at runtime — not in Hypothesis.__post_init__, not in canonical_hypothesis_digest, not in _resolve_direction, and not in the confirm functions. The Literal is type-checker-only. In _empirical_p / _resample_stability the value is dispatched as `if tail == 'greater': ... else: <less-tail>`, so ANY value other than the exact string 'greater' is silently treated as the 'less' (opposite) tail. PoC (verified against the venv): a hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='up' (a plausible adapter typo) passes lock_hypothesis + require_preregistered cleanly (the digest binds the typo), then confirm_whole_corpus on an observation deep in the GREATER tail returns CONTRADICTED with p_support=1.0 — the exact inverse of the correct SUPPORTED/p_support=0.0098 result — and records `predicted_direction: 'up'` in the verdict evidence as if legitimate. This silently corrupts the central scientific output of the engine (the three verdicts) on a minor adapter mistake, with no error anywhere. This is a missing runtime check a misimplemented adapter critically needs.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate predicted_direction in one canonical place. Either reject non-{'greater','less'} in Hypothesis.__post_init__ (raise ValueError listing the legal values), or — better, since pre-registration is the security boundary — reject it in canonical_hypothesis_digest/lock_hypothesis so an invalid direction can never be locked. Additionally make _resolve_direction/_empirical_p raise on an unrecognized tail rather than defaulting to the 'less' branch (`if tail not in ('greater','less'): raise ValueError`).

findings.title: Verdict.label is unvalidated; a non-enum label crashes scorecard aggregation with an opaque KeyError
findings.description: Verdict.__post_init__ freezes evidence but never checks that `label` is a VerdictLabel. A study-supplied ClaimConfirmer/HeldOutConfirmer (the public Protocols in adjudication.py / discovery.py) returns a Verdict; an adapter that builds one with a bare string label (label='supported') — or via dataclasses.replace — produces a structurally-valid frozen Verdict. _score then does `counts[v.label] += 1` against a dict keyed only by the three VerdictLabel members, raising KeyError('supported') instead of a contract error. PoC verified: Verdict('h1', label='supported', 'rule') feeds _score → KeyError('supported'). In run_study this surfaces deep in the ADJUDICATE/SCORE phase as an opaque KeyError rather than a typed FirewallViolation/ValueError naming the offending hypothesis, and the pipeline's verdict.label.value provenance lines (pipeline.py:438,488) would AttributeError on a string. The contract gives adapter authors no runtime feedback that they returned the wrong type.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if not isinstance(self.label, VerdictLabel): raise TypeError(...)` to Verdict.__post_init__ (and similarly validate hypothesis_id is a non-empty str). This converts a misimplemented confirmer into an immediate, attributable contract error at Verdict construction rather than an opaque KeyError during scoring.

findings.title: Parser returning a non-Corpus escapes IngestionError normalization (opaque AttributeError)
findings.description: run_study's docstring promises 'Raises IngestionError on a bad source', and the INGEST try/except (lines 292-296) normalizes any exception from parser.parse()/source_fingerprint() into IngestionError. But the result is not type-checked: `if not corpus.units` (line 297) and `corpus_fingerprint(corpus)` (line 299) run OUTSIDE that try. An IngestionParser that returns None / a list / a dict (a common adapter misimplementation, since the Protocol's `parse` return type is unenforced) does not raise inside parse, so it slips past the normalizer and dies at `corpus.units` with `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'units'`. PoC verified end-to-end through run_study. The engine offers no isinstance(corpus, Corpus) guard — precisely the 'missing runtime check a misimplemented adapter would need' for this dimension, and a documented-contract violation (the promised IngestionError is not what's raised).
findings.suggested_fix: Inside the existing try (or immediately after), assert the type: `if not isinstance(corpus, Corpus): raise IngestionError(f'parser returned {type(corpus).__name__}, expected Corpus')`. Do the same for source_fingerprint returning a non-str. This keeps the documented IngestionError contract honest for the most common adapter mistake.

findings.title: Frozen StudyDefinition (and peer records) accept a mutable set for frozenset-typed fields, breaking immutability/hashability
findings.description: StudyDefinition is @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) and declares modes: frozenset[StudyMode], implying a hashable, immutable value object (it is used as a registry-keyed, pre-registration-anchoring record). But __post_init__ never coerces/validates modes to a frozenset, so passing a plain set (which membership-checks identically, so all internal `StudyMode.X in modes` logic and required_phases(modes) work) is silently accepted. PoC verified: StudyDefinition(..., modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}) yields .modes of type set, and hash(definition) then raises `TypeError: unhashable type: 'set'` — a 'frozen' record that is neither hashable nor immutable, and is also mutable in place. The codebase's own test_contract.py:60 constructs StudyDefinition with claims_source=object() relying on direct construction, and the discovery() factory is the only path that coerces to frozenset; the raw constructor (the public API surface a clone uses) does not. DiscoverConfirmSplit (frozenset fields) has the same un-coerced-constructor shape, though from_partition coerces.
findings.suggested_fix: In StudyDefinition.__post_init__ coerce/validate: `object.__setattr__(self, 'modes', frozenset(self.modes))` (and ideally check each element is a StudyMode). Apply the same frozenset coercion in DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ so the documented frozen+hashable contract holds regardless of which constructor a clone uses.

findings.title: Inconsistent @runtime_checkable usage: seam Protocols give name-only validation; adapter contract Protocols give none
findings.description: The seam Protocols (DataVersioner, Tracer, ExperimentTracker, VectorStore, the reasoning seam) are decorated @runtime_checkable, but the core engine↔adapter contract Protocols a clone must implement — IngestionParser, ExternalClaimsSource, FeatureBuilder, BaselineBuilder, TimestampAuthority, HeldOutConfirmer, ClaimConfirmer — are NOT. This is a stability inconsistency with two distinct hazards: (1) isinstance against the adapter Protocols raises TypeError ('Instance and class checks can only be used with @runtime_checkable protocols'), so neither the engine nor a clone can defensively isinstance-check an adapter object — PoC verified. (2) The @runtime_checkable that DOES exist is a false-confidence trap: it validates method NAMES only, not signatures, so isinstance(WrongVersioner(), DataVersioner) returns True even when put()/fingerprint() take the wrong arguments (PoC verified). Meanwhile none of the @runtime_checkable seam Protocols are actually isinstance-checked anywhere in src/, so the decorator is currently dead weight that invites callers (and clones) to rely on a check that wouldn't catch a real misimplementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one consistent policy. Recommended: keep adapter validation behavior-based (call the methods, normalize failures to typed errors as the parser/Verdict fixes above do) and DROP the @runtime_checkable decorators that are never used, OR document explicitly that runtime_checkable is name-only and is not a substitute for signature/behavior validation. If isinstance-based adapter validation is desired, make the contract Protocols runtime_checkable too — but pair it with explicit call-time checks since names-only isinstance will not catch wrong signatures/return types.

findings.title: FeatureMatrix accepts non-numeric rows despite its numeric type contract
findings.description: FeatureMatrix declares rows: tuple[tuple[float, ...], ...] and the module docstring states output is 'a plain numeric FeatureMatrix ... so the engine consumes it without knowing what the features mean.' __post_init__ validates row COUNT and WIDTH but never that the cells are numeric. PoC verified: FeatureMatrix(unit_ids=('u1',), feature_names=('f',), rows=(('not-a-number',),)) is accepted. A FeatureBuilder adapter that emits strings/None therefore passes construction and run_study's ingest-time checks (which only validate unit_ids dedup/ghost ids, pipeline.py:330-340), letting non-numeric 'features' flow into any downstream numeric consumer and fail far from the source — a silent break on a minor adapter change, with the only contract signal being a type annotation the runtime ignores.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate cell types in FeatureMatrix.__post_init__: reject any cell that is not a real, finite number (e.g. `if not isinstance(x, (int, float)) or isinstance(x, bool) or not math.isfinite(x): raise ValueError(...)`), mirroring the finite-float discipline already enforced in confirm._validate_finite and the canonical hashing path.

findings.title: High-stakes options test never asserts env=scrubbed_env() is wired in — the no-metered-API firewall is unguarded
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py is the ONLY test that drives _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:361). Its fake _ClaudeAgentOptions stores all constructor kwargs in __dict__ (line 55) and its fake query() receives `options` (line 63), but NOT ONE test ever reads options.env. The credential-scrubbing firewall (ADR-0003 / 'no metered API') is the single most security-critical wiring in the codebase: _high_stakes_complete passes `env=scrubbed_env()` into ClaudeAgentOptions (seam.py:388) so the subprocess never inherits ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, off-box redirects (ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL), proxies, or AWS/GOOGLE creds. VERIFIED non-discriminating: deleting the entire `env=scrubbed_env(),` line from _high_stakes_complete leaves all 12 drain tests AND the full 78-test reasoning suite green. scrubbed_env() is unit-tested in isolation (test_reasoning.py:71) but its USE — that the scrubbed env actually reaches the subprocess options — is not. A refactor that drops/renames the env kwarg would ship a metered-credential leak with a green CI.
findings.suggested_fix: In the fake_sdk fixture, capture the ClaudeAgentOptions instance (e.g. record options in the query() closure or a module-level holder) and add assertions: that options.env is exactly scrubbed_env() (contains the OAuth token but every unsafe var == ''), specifically that a monkeypatched ANTHROPIC_API_KEY appears as '' in options.env. This makes the no-metered-API guarantee discriminating end-to-end, not just at the pure-function level.

findings.title: High-stakes subprocess sandbox settings (permission_mode/allowed_tools/setting_sources) are never asserted
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: _high_stakes_complete builds ClaudeAgentOptions with permission_mode='dontAsk', setting_sources=[], allowed_tools=[] (seam.py:382-389) — the sandbox that stops the subscription subprocess from executing arbitrary tools or reading project/user settings. No drain test reads any of these option fields. VERIFIED non-discriminating: changing permission_mode to 'bypassPermissions' and allowed_tools to ['Bash'] (which would let the high-stakes Claude subprocess run arbitrary shell commands on the production node) passes all 12 drain tests. A regression weakening the sandbox would be invisible to CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Capture the constructed ClaudeAgentOptions in the fixture and assert permission_mode == 'dontAsk', allowed_tools == [], and setting_sources == [] (and system_prompt/model are threaded through). Pair with the env assertion above so the whole ADR-0003 hardening of the subprocess is locked down by tests.

findings.title: Pipeline lock-before-baseline ordering (not_after=baseline_instant) is not tested through run_study
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: The runner captures baseline_instant BEFORE the blind baseline build (pipeline.py:350) and passes it as not_after to adjudicate_with_baseline (pipeline.py:501). This is the methodology-critical guarantee that a claim-derived (EXTERNAL_CLAIM) hypothesis must have been pre-registered before the baseline/answer existed, so it cannot be tuned to the baseline (#83). VERIFIED non-discriminating at the pipeline layer: changing `not_after=baseline_instant` to `not_after=_now()` in run_study (which would accept a hypothesis locked AFTER the baseline) leaves all 38 pipeline + e2e + live-pipeline tests green. The guarantee is only exercised at the adjudicate() UNIT level (test_adjudication.py:235 test_runner_rejects_hypothesis_locked_after_baseline) — never through run_study, which is the layer that wires baseline_instant. The reference adapter pre-locks hypotheses an hour in the past, so the boundary is never hit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a run_study adjudicate-mode test whose hypothesis_for locks the claim hypothesis on demand at now() (after the baseline build) and assert run_study raises PreRegistrationError/FirewallViolation. This makes the pipeline-level wiring of baseline_instant discriminating, mirroring test_runner_rejects_hypothesis_locked_after_baseline but through the composed runner.

findings.title: verdict_from_pvalue significance boundary (p_value == alpha) is untested
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: test_verdict_mapping_covers_three_outcomes only uses clearly-significant (p=0.01 vs alpha=0.05) and clearly-non-significant (p=0.4) inputs, so the inclusive boundary in `if p_value <= alpha` (confirm.py:130) is never pinned. VERIFIED non-discriminating: flipping `p_value <= alpha` to `p_value < alpha` (making a p-value exactly equal to alpha NOT significant — a real change to the decision rule at the threshold) passes the entire confirm + preregistration suite. The same untested boundary applies to the empirical-p comparisons in confirm_whole_corpus (p_contra <= alpha / p_support <= alpha).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a case with p_value exactly == alpha (e.g. p=0.05, alpha=0.05) asserting SUPPORTED (inclusive boundary), and a whole-corpus case where the empirical p lands exactly on alpha, to lock the documented '<= alpha' rule.

findings.title: QdrantVectorStore.query path is never exercised
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: The vector store tests cover upsert batching, length-mismatch, client ownership/close, and loopback enforcement, but QdrantVectorStore.query (vectorstore.py:110-114) — which reads p.payload['ref'] and float(p.score) and is the read side of the store — has zero coverage. A regression mangling the payload-ref mapping or the score coercion would go uncaught. Lower priority because it is a goal-agnostic reference implementation a study may replace.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test using a FakeClient.query_points returning synthetic points with payload {'ref': ...} and a score, asserting query() returns the [(ref, score)] tuples (and round-trips the _point_id/payload mapping used by upsert).

findings.title: Concurrent setup() once-per-conn guard is asserted only sequentially, not under real threads
findings.dimension: test quality / do tests actually guard behavior
findings.description: checkpoint.py documents that the in-process _init_lock makes the bootstrap check-and-set atomic 'so concurrent threads don't both run setup' (checkpoint.py:22-23, 195). test_setup_runs_once_per_conn_and_on_locked_connection calls get_checkpointer() twice SEQUENTIALLY, which exercises only the once-per-conn dedup, not the concurrency claim — two threads racing into the `if conn_str not in _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS` window would still be serialized by GIL+narrow-window even without the lock, but the test cannot demonstrate the lock matters. Unlike the provenance concurrency test (test_provenance.py:170, which injects a sleeping clock to widen the race and was verified discriminating by removing the lock), there is no thread-level test that the checkpoint _init_lock is load-bearing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a threaded test that launches several get_checkpointer() calls concurrently (with the fake_pg backend, optionally a small sleep inside FakeSaver.setup to widen the window) and asserts setup() still ran exactly once. Verify it is discriminating by confirming it fails if the `with _init_lock:` around the bootstrap block is removed.

findings.title: Recorded claim_fingerprint is never bound to the claims actually adjudicated/scored — the gate and provenance attest to a value the source self-reports
findings.description: In the adjudication path run_study materializes claims with `claim_records = list(defn.claims_source.claims())` (line 452) and, separately, records `claim_fingerprint=defn.claims_source.claim_fingerprint()` into the human-gate review payload and (via the gate) the provenance trail (line 467). The engine NEVER computes its own content hash of the materialized `claim_records`, and never checks the source-reported fingerprint against the claims it actually scores. The fingerprint is a self-report the source can make say anything. Pass-1 issue #95 hardened against a re-iterable source by materializing claims once, but left the *recorded fingerprint* disconnected from that materialized snapshot. CONCRETE PoC (run through run_study): a claims source whose `claim_fingerprint()` returns the SHA-256 of an innocent 2-claim set 'c1,c2' (442183c741eed348...) while `claims()` yields 3 claims including 'c3_injected'. The run completes; the gate review and provenance record fingerprint 442183c741eed348 (the innocent 2-claim hash) while the SourceScorecard scores 3 claims (H-c1, H-c2, H-c3_injected). An auditor re-deriving the fingerprint from the recorded/scored claims gets a DIFFERENT value than the trail records, and a human gate approving on the basis of the fingerprint is misled. The provenance 'spine of reproducibility' (GOVERNANCE §3) thus carries a decorative, unverifiable claim identity. Note the claim_ids list IS shown, but the cryptographic binding the fingerprint is supposed to provide is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Have the engine compute the claim-set fingerprint itself over the materialized `claim_records` using the existing type-faithful serializer (e.g. hash_value(canonical_json) over a canonicalized list of ClaimRecord fields), record THAT engine-computed value into the gate payload and provenance, and—if the source also exposes claim_fingerprint()—assert the source's value equals the engine-computed one, raising FirewallViolation on mismatch. This makes the recorded fingerprint provably the hash of the claims actually scored.

findings.title: No claim/hypothesis identity uniqueness guard — duplicate claim_ids inflate the SourceScorecard denominator and bias alignment_rate
findings.description: adjudicate_with_baseline iterates `for claim in list(claims)` and appends one verdict per claim with no check that claim_ids (or the resulting hypothesis_ids) are unique within the run. _score() then counts every verdict, so the SAME claim scored N times counts N times in n_supported/n_contradicted/n_indeterminate and in the alignment_rate denominator. The scorecard docstring defines alignment_rate as 'the fraction of *decisive claims*'—an identity-level frequency—but identity is never enforced, so a source can bias the rate by repeating a claim. The discovery runner and the pipeline discovery loop have the same gap for duplicate hypothesis_ids. CONCRETE PoC (run through run_study): a claims source yielding claim_id 'c1' three times produces scorecard total=3, n_supported=3, alignment_rate=1.0 — the single claim c1 inflated the denominator 3x with no error. This silently corrupts the §5 'score the source' metric and the verdict-per-claim accounting.
findings.suggested_fix: Enforce within-run identity uniqueness: in adjudicate_with_baseline track seen claim_ids and raise FirewallViolation on a repeat (or dedup deterministically and record the collapse); likewise track hypothesis_ids in the discovery loop and in pipeline's confirm loop. Identity (claim<->hypothesis<->verdict) is already a first-class invariant elsewhere in these runners; uniqueness is the missing half.

findings.title: Whole-corpus 'stability across resamples' is measured against the null median, not the observed statistic — resamples disconnected from the observed effect earn stability=1.0
findings.description: confirm_whole_corpus computes stability as `_resample_stability(resample_statistics, median(null_distribution), direction)` — the fraction of resamples on the predicted side of the NULL MEDIAN, not of the OBSERVED statistic. The docstring claims this measures 'the effect direction reproduces across resamples' and gates SUPPORTED on it (audit pass-1 issue #21 made stability engine-measured rather than caller-asserted, but used the wrong reference point). Because the reference is the null center, resamples that barely exceed the null median count as fully 'stable' even when they are nowhere near the observed effect — so 'the observed effect reproduces' is not what is actually tested. CONCRETE PoC: null=range(0,21) (median 10), observed=100 (significant, p_support=0.045), resamples=[10.0001]*100 (just above the null median, far below the observed 100). confirm_whole_corpus returns SUPPORTED with stability=1.0, despite the resamples reproducing nothing of the size-100 effect. The existing tests only use resamples at +/-100 (far from the null), so the flaw is never exercised. This weakens (does not fully defeat — resamples must still land on the predicted side) the stability guard that gates the SUPPORTED verdict.
findings.suggested_fix: Measure stability relative to the observed statistic's relationship to the null, not the null median alone — e.g. compute each resample's own empirical p (or sign of effect vs the null) and require the fraction that remain significant/extreme in the predicted tail to meet stability_threshold, so 'stability' reflects reproduction of the observed effect, not merely being on the predicted side of the null center.

findings.title: Measurement<->Interpretation fence and freeze() both bypassed by a duck-typed (non-abc.Mapping) mapping — yields a mutable 'frozen' Measurement that contains an Interpretation
findings.description: _contains_interpretation recurses only into collections.abc.Mapping and native list/tuple/set/frozenset; freeze() likewise deep-converts only those types. A duck-typed mapping (exposes items()/keys()/__iter__ but is NOT registered as collections.abc.Mapping — a common shape for lazy/proxy dict-likes in adapter code) is treated as an opaque leaf by both: the fence scan does not look inside it, and freeze() leaves it as the live, mutable object (it is hashable by identity, so freeze's final hash() check passes it through). Pass-1 issue #115 closed the Interpretation-as-dict-key vector for real mappings but missed the duck-mapping vector. CONCRETE PoC: class DuckMap with items/keys/__iter__ over {'k': interp}; Measurement(value=DuckMap(...)) constructs successfully (one-way fence breached — an Interpretation now sits inside a Measurement), m.value IS the original mutable DuckMap (not a FrozenDict), and m.value._d['k']='MUTATED' mutates a supposedly frozen Measurement payload after construction. So both the fence's one-way direction (METHODOLOGY §6) and the deep-immutability contract (_frozen module purpose) are defeated by the same input.
findings.suggested_fix: Make both checks structural over the duck-typed mapping shape, not nominal: in freeze() and _contains_interpretation treat any object exposing keys()+__getitem__ (or items()) as a mapping (convert/recurse accordingly), or reject leaves that are mapping-like but not a recognized immutable type. Aligning freeze() and the fence scan on the same broadened mapping detection closes both the immutability and the one-way-fence bypass together.

findings.title: Data-sovereignty over-claim: the optional high-stakes reasoning tier sends prompts off-box, yet docs guarantee "nothing leaves the machine" unconditionally
findings.description: Multiple normative docs state an absolute data-residency guarantee: README L71 "Everything runs on-box (local-first, data-sovereign); nothing leaves the machine."; CHARTER §2.5 "No data leaves the machine."; GOVERNANCE §5 "No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine."; and ADR-0003 L16-17 "The high-stakes reasoning tier uses a fixed-cost subscription path (no metered API)... No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine." But the code's high-stakes tier provably sends content off-box. src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:361-439 (_high_stakes_complete) imports claude_agent_sdk and runs `query(prompt=prompt, ...)` through the `claude` CLI subprocess, which calls Anthropic's frontier model over the network. The docstring (seam.py:9-12) itself says HIGH_STAKES = "frontier model via a fixed-cost subscription path (the claude CLI / Agent SDK using an OAuth token)." The docs conflate two distinct concerns: "no metered API" (a billing model) is NOT the same as "on-box" (data residency). Sending a prompt to a frontier model is inherently off-box. scrubbed_env() (seam.py:160-174) scrubs metered credentials and proxies, but the OAuth subscription call still reaches Anthropic. ADR-0003 L23 "runnable fully offline; nothing depends on a remote service to advance" is true only for the bulk/local tier and the methodology core, not the high-stakes tier. A reviewer or operator relying on the unconditional guarantee for sensitive/licensed data would be misled: if a study's adapter callable invokes the HIGH_STAKES tier, prompt content (derived from the data) leaves the box.
findings.suggested_fix: Qualify the absolute guarantee everywhere it appears. State that the on-box guarantee covers all engine computation, storage, tracing, and experiment tracking AND the BULK/local reasoning tier; and that the optional HIGH_STAKES frontier tier sends prompt content to Anthropic over the network (fixed-cost subscription, not metered, but off-box). Recommend wording like: "All engine computation, storage, observability, and the local (bulk) reasoning tier run on-box. The optional high-stakes tier routes prompts to a frontier model via the subscription CLI — content for that tier leaves the box; a study handling data that must never leave should use only the bulk tier or UnconfiguredReasoningSeam." Update ADR-0003 to separate the billing claim (no metered API) from the residency claim.

findings.title: CHARTER status says infrastructure seams are "stubs being wired" — contradicts README/ADR-0010 and the actual (implemented, tested) code
findings.description: CHARTER.md L3 status line reads: "Status: Active development — engine skeleton and methodology core implemented (see ARCHITECTURE.md / ADR-0002); infrastructure seams are stubs being wired." This is stale and contradicts both the README and the code. README L10-15 states the seams are "in place and hardened ... all five infrastructure seams (tiered reasoning, self-hosted observability, persistence, orchestration, baseline toolkit) ... a single composed runner — run_study." The code confirms the README, not the CHARTER: reasoning/seam.py, observability/tracing.py + tracking.py, persistence/{checkpoint,vectorstore,versioning}.py, orchestration/{graph,gates,gatenode,phases}.py, and baseline/* are all fully implemented (not stubs), with run_study composing them and 407 tests passing (5 skipped). ADR-0010 (Accepted 2026-06-16) documents the seams as "ported and individually live-verified" and composed. The CHARTER status is the only doc still describing the seams as unfinished stubs.
findings.suggested_fix: Update CHARTER.md L3 status to match reality and the README, e.g. "Status: Engine implemented and composed — methodology core, all five infrastructure seams, append-only provenance, and the composed run_study runner are in place and hardened (ADR-0010)."

findings.title: ARCHITECTURE.md and package docstring claim the engine ships embedding/clustering/graph/topology builders; it ships only similarity/distance + descriptive stats (the modules' own docstrings say so)
findings.description: ARCHITECTURE §1 "Baseline toolkit" row claims the engine provides "Generic, dataset-agnostic builders: embeddings, similarity/distance, graph/topology, clustering, descriptive statistics. The raw material for any baseline." The public package docstring (src/assay_engine/__init__.py:14-15) repeats it: "baseline — dataset-agnostic baseline builders (embeddings, similarity, graph, clustering, descriptive stats)." But the engine ships NO embedding, clustering, graph, or topology builders. baseline/primitives.py provides only cosine/euclidean similarity-distance and descriptive_stats; baseline/determinism.py provides the determinism harness. The modules' OWN docstrings explicitly contradict the ARCHITECTURE/__init__ claim: primitives.py:8-12 "Studies needing performance or richer ML (embedding models, clustering, graph/topology) bring their own builders... the engine deliberately does not prescribe those choices (ADR-0002)."; toolkit.py:4-7 "the choice-bearing builder that assembles them — which embedding model, which clustering, which graph — encodes dataset decisions and is supplied by a study's adapter." grep confirms no kmeans/cluster/networkx/embed/graph builder anywhere in src/. So the engine-component description over-states what is shipped, and the package's public docstring is internally self-contradictory with its own submodule docstrings.
findings.suggested_fix: Correct ARCHITECTURE §1 and __init__.py:14-15 to describe what the engine actually ships, e.g. "Baseline toolkit: dataset-agnostic primitives (similarity/distance, descriptive statistics) and a determinism/reproducibility harness — the raw material; the choice-bearing builders (embeddings, clustering, graph/topology) are supplied per-study by the adapter's BaselineBuilder (ADR-0002)."

findings.title: METHODOLOGY §7 and GOVERNANCE §2 body text claim pre-registration is "RFC-3161 timestamped"; the engine ships only an HMAC authority (RFC-3161 is pluggable, not shipped/default)
findings.description: Normative body text presents RFC-3161 timestamping as the mechanism actually in force. METHODOLOGY §7 L130-132: "Pre-registration: hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation." GOVERNANCE §2 L55-62 step 3: hypotheses are "RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority." But the engine ships exactly one timestamp authority — LocalHmacAuthority (on-box HMAC) — and no RFC-3161 TSA client (grep of src/ finds RFC-3161 only in comments/docstrings as a pluggable option; preregistration.py:130 LocalHmacAuthority is the only concrete authority). The runnable example (examples/minimal_study.py:38) and all defaults use LocalHmacAuthority. GOVERNANCE §2's own implementation-status note (L64-81) and ADR-0009 correctly hedge this ("ships one real, data-sovereign authority LocalHmacAuthority... and the Protocol so a study can plug an RFC-3161 TSA"), but the §7/§2 body claims read as if RFC-3161 (third-party non-repudiation) is what happens. HMAC is local tamper-evidence relative to an on-box secret — explicitly NOT the third-party time attestation RFC-3161 provides. The mismatch matters because RFC-3161 is exactly the non-repudiation property a hostile-reviewer audience would key on.
findings.suggested_fix: In the METHODOLOGY §7 bullet and GOVERNANCE §2 step 3, change the flat "RFC-3161 timestamped" to the shipped default with the pluggable option, e.g. "locked and cryptographically timestamped before confirmation (on-box HMAC by default — local tamper-evidence; an RFC-3161 TSA is pluggable for third-party non-repudiation)." Keep the body text consistent with the existing honest-scope notes.

findings.title: GOVERNANCE §1 lists five canonical gate transitions (incl. pre-analysis checkpoint, reporting, escalation) as guarded; the engine runner wires only two confirmatory gates
findings.description: GOVERNANCE §1 enumerates five "canonical transitions a gate guards": pre-analysis checkpoint, lock/pre-registration, review, reporting, and escalation, describing each as a code-enforced gate that "cannot be silently bypassed." The composed runner run_study wires only TWO gates: review-locked-hypotheses (PREREGISTER→CONFIRM, pipeline.py:412) and review-baseline-and-claims (→ADJUDICATE, pipeline.py:458). There is NO pre-analysis-checkpoint gate before INGEST, NO reporting gate before REPORT (confirmed results flow straight into REPORT at pipeline.py:526-533 with no operator review), and NO escalation gate. GOVERNANCE §1 does hedge with "the exact count and labels are an engine convention" and "A concrete study maps its phases onto these transitions," and ADR-0010 §4 is explicit ("There are two gates"). But §1's specific claim that the "Reporting" transition — "confirmed results are reviewed before anything is framed as a conclusion or leaves the analytical layer" — is gated is not honored by the engine; the README's "block/park before each confirmatory step" is accurate, but §1 presents reporting/pre-analysis/escalation as engine-guarded gates they are not.
findings.suggested_fix: Reconcile GOVERNANCE §1 with the runner: state clearly which gates the engine's run_study enforces (the two confirmatory gates) versus which transitions are conventions a study may add via the LangGraph make_gate_node path. Either add a reporting/pre-analysis gate to run_study or relabel §1's list as "transitions a study MAY gate" with an explicit note that the engine runner enforces gates before each confirmatory step only.

findings.title: README quickstart leads with `pip install assay-engine`, but the package is not published on PyPI (404)
findings.description: The README Quickstart's first instruction is `pip install assay-engine` / `pip install "assay-engine[all]"` (L24-25), presented as the primary install path with no caveat. The distribution name is assay-engine (pyproject.toml:6) but it is not on PyPI — https://pypi.org/pypi/assay-engine/json returns HTTP 404. A user copy-pasting the quickstart cannot install the package; the only working path is an editable/source install (CONTRIBUTING.md correctly uses `pip install -e ".[dev]"`). The runnable example works from source (verified: `python examples/minimal_study.py` succeeds), so the methodology demo is fine — but the headline install command is non-functional for a production/public reader. For a Beta (Development Status :: 4) package this is a real onboarding over-claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Either publish assay-engine to PyPI, or until then add a note in the Quickstart that the package is not yet on PyPI and show the from-source install (`pip install -e .` after cloning), matching CONTRIBUTING.md. At minimum mark the pip line as the post-publication path.

findings.title: ADR-0006 enumerates the install extras as four names and an install line that omits the `baseline` extra (numpy)
findings.description: ADR-0006 §1 lists the named install extras as "(reasoning, observability, persistence, orchestration)" and the Consequences install example as `pip install '.[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration]'` (L40), both omitting the `baseline` extra. pyproject.toml defines a fifth optional extra `baseline = ["numpy..."]` (L68-70) and includes it in `all` (L72-74); the README correctly lists "baseline" among the extras (L17) and primitives.py dispatches to a numpy path under that extra. So ADR-0006's enumeration and example install command are stale relative to the shipped extras set. Low impact (numpy is a pure-Python-fallback accelerator, not required), but the ADR's normative list of extras is incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `baseline` to ADR-0006 §1's extras enumeration and to the §Consequences install example (or reference `[all]`), so the ADR matches pyproject.toml's five extras.

findings.title: pip-audit CI job omits --strict, so silently-skipped (unauditable) deps pass the supply-chain gate
findings.description: The audit job's stated purpose is to "fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship with advisories)". But the invocation `pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES` does NOT pass `--strict`/`-S`. I confirmed in the venv (.venv/bin/pip-audit 2.10.1) that without --strict pip-audit exits 0 even when a dependency cannot be collected/audited, and exits 1 with --strict: `pip-audit --progress-spinner off` -> exit 0; `pip-audit --progress-spinner off --strict` -> exit 1. The job ALREADY hits this: every run prints `assay-engine ... Dependency not found on PyPI and could not be audited` and still passes. The same silent-skip occurs for any transitive package that fails advisory-DB collection (a transient OSV/PyPI hiccup, a yanked/private package, or a future dep not on PyPI). Result: a dependency can go completely un-scanned while CI reports green, defeating the gate. For a security-branded engine this is a real coverage hole, not style.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `-S`/`--strict` to the pip-audit invocation so any failure to collect/audit a dependency fails the job. If the local `assay-engine` self-package must be excluded, audit a generated requirements/lockfile of the third-party deps instead of the live env (see lockfile finding), rather than tolerating arbitrary silent skips.

findings.title: .pip-audit-ignore parser drops the last advisory ID when the file has no trailing newline
findings.description: The allowlist is parsed with `while read -r id; do ...; done < .pip-audit-ignore`. POSIX `read` returns non-zero on a final line with no trailing newline, so the loop body never runs for that line and the ID is silently dropped. I reproduced this: file content `printf 'GHSA-first-line-aaaa\nPYSEC-2024-last-no-newline'` yields parsed IGNORES = `--ignore-vuln GHSA-first-line-aaaa` only — the last ID is MISSING. The common case is a single-line allowlist (one triaged/unfixable advisory). Many editors and tools (`echo -n`, `printf` without \n, some web edits) produce no final newline, so the very advisory a maintainer adds to keep CI green is silently NOT suppressed and the build fails on an already-triaged finding — making the documented allowlist mechanism unreliable. The file itself does not yet exist in the repo (no `.pip-audit-ignore` tracked or untracked), so this latent bug is also completely untested.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the read loop newline-agnostic and comment/blank tolerant, e.g. `while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do ... done < .pip-audit-ignore` and skip lines matching `^#` or empty. Add a tiny test/fixture `.pip-audit-ignore` so the path is exercised. Better: drop the hand-rolled loop and feed a hashed/locked requirements file to pip-audit, or build the `--ignore-vuln` list with a parser that strips comments and trims whitespace.

findings.title: Unquoted $IGNORES expansion word-splits and glob-expands allowlist lines
findings.description: `IGNORES` is accumulated as a single string and passed unquoted: `pip-audit ... $IGNORES`. Real advisory IDs (GHSA-*, PYSEC-*, CVE-*) are word-split-safe, so today it works for valid input — but the ci.yml comment invites "one advisory id per line" without forbidding comments, and any line containing spaces (e.g. an accidental `# comment`) is split into multiple bogus `--ignore-vuln` tokens. I reproduced: a `# a comment line` line becomes `--ignore-vuln # a comment line` (4 tokens). pip-audit happens to accept unknown ignore IDs without erroring (I confirmed `--ignore-vuln '# a comment line'` exits 0), so it fails open silently rather than loudly. Combined with the unquoted expansion, a stray glob char in the file could also undergo pathname expansion. This is fragile, fail-open input handling in the security gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Build an array instead of a string: `args=(); while ...; do args+=(--ignore-vuln "$id"); done` then `pip-audit "${args[@]}"`. Strip comments/whitespace and validate each ID against an expected pattern (e.g. `^(GHSA|PYSEC|CVE)-`).

findings.title: No lockfile / constraints / hashes — supply-chain audit and installs are non-reproducible
findings.description: Pass-1 finding #8 added pip-audit to CI but left the reproducibility half of its own suggested fix unaddressed: there is still NO lockfile (no uv.lock/poetry.lock), NO constraints*.txt, NO requirements*.txt, and the audit job runs `pip install -e ".[dev,all]"` resolving fresh versions every run. I confirmed the heavy extras pull a large, fast-moving surface (217+ installed dists; current resolution includes cryptography 48.0.1, aiohttp 3.14.1, requests 2.34.2, urllib3 2.7.0, Werkzeug 3.1.8, Flask 3.1.3, grpcio 1.81.1, protobuf 6.33.6, openai 2.41.1, anthropic 0.109.2, boto3 1.43.30, SQLAlchemy 2.0.51). Because nothing is pinned, (a) the pip-audit result is a point-in-time snapshot that can differ between two CI runs on the same commit, (b) a green audit today gives no guarantee about the artifact a user installs tomorrow, and (c) pip-audit's hash/pinned-aware checks (`--require-hashes`, `--locked`) cannot be used. pip-audit clean today (OSV and PyPI both: 'No known vulnerabilities found') — but that is exactly the transient guarantee the lockfile gap undermines.
findings.suggested_fix: Commit a locked/constraints file for the heavy lanes (e.g. `uv pip compile` or `pip-compile` output, or `uv.lock`) and have the audit job install from it and run `pip-audit -r <locked-file> --require-hashes -S`, while keeping pyproject ranges permissive for library consumers. This makes both installs and the advisory scan reproducible.

findings.title: Over-wide version range for arize-phoenix (>=7.0,<18) admits 10 major versions
findings.description: `arize-phoenix>=7.0,<18` spans eleven major versions (7 through 17). The installed/resolved version is 17.6.0, ten majors above the floor. A range this wide defeats the point of the cap: any breaking change or a freshly-disclosed advisory in any major 7-17 is admitted by a clean install, and the engine has no lockfile to constrain it (see lockfile finding). Other extras are reasonably bounded (single-major caps), making this one an outlier. Same family is also slightly inconsistent: langgraph>=1.2,<2 is duplicated across the `persistence` and `orchestration` extras (functionally fine, but two sources of truth for one bound).
findings.suggested_fix: Tighten the phoenix cap to the majors actually tested (e.g. `>=17,<18`, matching the resolved 17.6.0) or whatever range integration CI truly exercises, and de-duplicate the langgraph bound (define it once and reference it) to avoid drift.

findings.title: No SBOM emission or automated dependency/license/transitive-monitoring in CI
findings.description: For a production security/privacy engine, the supply-chain story is point-in-time advisory scanning only. There is no SBOM artifact (pip-audit supports `-f cyclonedx-json`/`cyclonedx-xml` and cyclonedx-python-lib is already pulled in transitively), no `.github/dependabot.yml` (no automated transitive-update/PR monitoring), and no automated license check. I scanned all 230 installed dists for strong copyleft: the only two 'gpl' substring hits were false positives (matplotlib = PSF, pandas = BSD-3-Clause), so the license posture is currently clean — but nothing in CI would catch a future transitive dep introducing a GPL/AGPL component into this Apache-2.0 project, or a newly-disclosed CVE between PRs.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit and archive an SBOM in the audit job (`pip-audit -f cyclonedx-json -o sbom.json` as a CI artifact), add a `.github/dependabot.yml` (pip ecosystem) for transitive-update PRs, and add a lightweight license allowlist check (e.g. pip-licenses or cyclonedx license gate) so copyleft creep fails CI.

findings.title: HIGH_STAKES tier has no inner timeout/cancellation — hung subprocess calls permanently leak the global pool's worker slots, killing reasoning process-wide for ALL concurrent studies
findings.description: The reasoning seam routes every LLM call through a single module-global, 8-worker ThreadPoolExecutor (_pool) shared across all threads and all concurrent run_study invocations. _with_timeout enforces only a WALL-CLOCK timeout via future.result(timeout=...); it explicitly does NOT cancel the worker (the docstring at lines 226-229 acknowledges a ThreadPoolExecutor cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call). The slot is only released by the add_done_callback (_release), i.e. when the underlying call actually returns. For the BULK tier this is survivable because _bulk_complete passes client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT} to ChatOllama, so the worker is guaranteed to return and free its slot. But the HIGH_STAKES tier (_high_stakes_complete) passes NO timeout to ClaudeAgentOptions or query() and wraps the claude subprocess in anyio.run(_drain) with no cancellation. If the claude CLI subprocess hangs (stalled subscription endpoint, network blackhole, child process wedged), the worker thread blocks forever and its pool slot is NEVER released. _inflight stays elevated permanently. Once 8 such high-stakes calls hang — entirely plausible across concurrent studies when the subscription path stalls — the global pool is permanently saturated: _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError for EVERY subsequent call (any tier, any study), and _run_with_retries exhausts the rate-limit budget and fails the caller permanently. There is no recovery short of restarting the process. PoC confirmed: (1) 8 hung high-stakes-style calls drive seam._inflight to 8 and it never returns to 0; (2) an unrelated, healthy Study B issuing a fast call gets RateLimitError instead of running ('Study B cannot reason at all despite its own backend being fine'); (3) with _inflight forced to 8, seam._run_with_retries() on a fresh BULK request fails with RateLimitError after exhausting all rate retries in ~0.4s. This is a process-wide, cross-study, unrecoverable resource-exhaustion (effectively a livelock/deadlock of the whole reasoning subsystem) triggered by the most critical/gated tier.
findings.suggested_fix: Give the HIGH_STAKES path a real, enforceable timeout so a hung subprocess cannot permanently hold a worker. Run query()/_drain under anyio's move_on_after/fail_after (or asyncio.wait_for) bounded by HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT inside _high_stakes_complete, and ensure the claude subprocess is actually terminated on cancellation (the worker must RETURN so _release fires and the slot frees). Additionally, consider isolating the high-stakes tier in its own bounded pool so a high-stakes stall cannot starve bulk-tier callers, and/or have _release/health logic reclaim slots whose worker has exceeded a hard ceiling. Optionally surface a metric/alert when _inflight stays pinned at _POOL_WORKERS.

findings.title: Checkpointer bootstrap holds the process-wide _init_lock across a blocking, unbounded cross-process pg_advisory_lock + setup() — head-of-line blocking that can stall ALL checkpointer initialization (even for unrelated databases) indefinitely
findings.description: get_checkpointer acquires the single process-global _init_lock (line 195) and, while holding it, performs blocking I/O: pool.connection() (line 197), SELECT pg_advisory_lock(...) (line 198), and PostgresSaver(...).setup() DDL (line 203). pg_advisory_lock is a SESSION-level lock that blocks INDEFINITELY if another process already holds the same key (_MIGRATION_LOCK_KEY is a single fixed constant for all schemas). Because _init_lock is a single process-wide mutex (not keyed by conn_str), one thread blocked inside this section — e.g. waiting on the advisory lock held by a stuck or crashed peer process — blocks EVERY other thread's get_checkpointer call, including initialization for entirely UNRELATED databases / conn_strs that have nothing to do with the contended lock. Under concurrent run_study invocations that each build a checkpointer, this serializes all bootstrap and can wedge the whole process on a single peer's stuck advisory lock. PoC confirmed: a thread mirroring the exact (with _init_lock: ... advisory-lock-wait) structure for db_A blocks a second thread initializing an unrelated db_B for the full duration of db_A's advisory-lock wait (db_B blocked ~0.62s on a wait that, in production, has no upper bound).
findings.suggested_fix: Do not hold a single global mutex across blocking cross-process I/O. Either (a) replace _init_lock with a per-conn_str in-process lock (dict of locks keyed by conn_str, guarded by a tiny meta-lock) so different databases bootstrap concurrently and only same-conn_str threads serialize; and/or (b) acquire pg_advisory_lock with a bounded timeout (pg_advisory_lock with a statement_timeout, or use pg_try_advisory_lock with retry/backoff) so a stuck peer cannot hang init forever; and only hold the in-process lock around the in-process check-and-set of _INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS, not around the DDL/advisory-lock wait.

findings.title: get_checkpointer creates and registers a new ConnectionPool on every call with no per-conn_str pool reuse — repeated calls (e.g. per study run) leak pools, each holding a live worker thread and a min_size connection
findings.description: The once-per-process dedup (_INITIALIZED_CONN_STRS) covers only the schema setup()/advisory-lock bootstrap, not pool creation. Every successful get_checkpointer() call constructs a fresh ConnectionPool (min_size=1, max_size=8) and appends it to _OPEN_POOLS via _register_pool_cleanup, with no lookup/reuse of an existing pool for the same conn_str. Each pool eagerly opens (open=True) and spins up a background maintenance worker thread plus at least one live Postgres connection. If callers invoke configured_checkpointer()/get_checkpointer() repeatedly — for example once per study run under sustained load rather than caching a single checkpointer — the process accumulates an unbounded set of pools, worker threads, and DB connections that are never closed until interpreter exit (_close_all_pools runs only at atexit). This is unbounded resource growth under load (thread and connection exhaustion over a long-lived server process). It is partly mitigated if callers cache the checkpointer, but the factory makes no such guarantee and the dedup structure suggests one-pool-per-conn_str was intended.
findings.suggested_fix: Cache one open pool per conn_str (e.g. a dict keyed by conn_str guarded by _init_lock) and return a PostgresSaver over the existing pool on subsequent calls, instead of creating and registering a new pool every time. Close/evict cached pools that fail liveness. This bounds threads/connections at one pool per distinct conn_str regardless of call frequency.

findings.title: GateReview payload deep-freeze (fix #113) breaks JSON serialization of the public handler payload
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #113 added GateReview.__post_init__ that replaces the payload Mapping with a deep-frozen FrozenDict (object.__setattr__('payload', freeze_mapping(self.payload))). GateReview is a PUBLIC, operator-facing extension type (exported from assay_engine/__init__.py:46,81; GateHandler = Callable[[GateReview], GateDecision] is the documented operator-review extension point). Before the fix, review.payload was a plain dict; now it is a FrozenDict, which is NOT JSON-serializable. Any custom GateHandler that does the most natural thing — serialize/log the payload for an operator-review UI (json.dumps(review.payload)) — now raises TypeError. Confirmed PoC: GateReview(...,payload={'hypothesis_ids':['h1','h2'],'meta':{'n':3}}) -> json.dumps(r.payload) raises 'TypeError: Object of type FrozenDict is not JSON serializable'. Secondary: freeze() raises TypeError at construction for any non-hashable leaf, so payloads that previously constructed fine (e.g. containing an arbitrary non-hashable object) now fail at GateReview(...) construction. This is a behavioral regression in a public API surface, introduced solely to make the record hashable.
findings.suggested_fix: Keep immutability without breaking JSON: either (a) freeze to a structure json.dumps handles (e.g. keep a plain immutable dict via MappingProxyType plus a separate hashing path), or (b) give FrozenDict a JSON path (register or expose a to_dict()/__json__ and document that handlers must call dict(review.payload) before json.dumps), or (c) document loudly in the GateHandler contract that payload is a FrozenDict and provide a convenience like review.payload_dict(). At minimum document the type change in the public API/changelog.

findings.title: build_baseline_artifact accepts an unverified corpus_hash (fix #119), silently decoupling the determinism record from the corpus
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #119 added a corpus_hash parameter so a caller can pass a precomputed fingerprint and skip recomputing it. The function trusts the value blindly: 'corpus_hash = corpus_hash if corpus_hash is not None else corpus_fingerprint(corpus)'. When supplied, the corpus argument is NEVER actually hashed — the passed string is recorded as BaselineArtifact.corpus_fingerprint AND folded into input_hashes['corpus'] which drives the deterministic seed (stable_seed). A stale/typo'd/mismatched corpus_hash therefore produces a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived seed describe a DIFFERENT corpus than the one passed, with no error. For a determinism/reproducibility harness this silently defeats the core guarantee (the record no longer binds the data it claims to). Confirmed PoC: build_baseline_artifact(corpus, contents=..., corpus_hash='deadbeef'*8) returns corpus_fingerprint='deadbeef...' and a different seed than the honest build, while corpus_fingerprint(corpus) is a completely different value. The pipeline happens to re-validate downstream (pipeline.py:356 raises on baseline.corpus_fingerprint != cfp), but build_baseline_artifact is a builder primitive usable standalone, where nothing catches the mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: When BOTH corpus and corpus_hash are provided, verify they agree: compute corpus_fingerprint(corpus) lazily only if needed, or assert corpus_hash == corpus_fingerprint(corpus) (cheap relative to the build) and raise ValueError on mismatch. Alternatively make corpus and corpus_hash mutually exclusive, or document that corpus_hash is fully trusted and is the caller's integrity responsibility.

findings.title: Tracing bootstrap/context entered outside the try (fix #122) can leak a started experiment-tracker run
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #122 inserted bootstrap_tracing() and trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) AFTER tracker.start_run() (line 271) but BEFORE the main 'try:' (line 288) whose 'finally' is the only place tracker.end_run() runs (line 559-562). If either bootstrap_tracing() or enter_context(run_trace_context(...)) raises, the tracker run was already started but the finally is never reached, so the MLflow run leaks in RUNNING state forever (the very class of bug #110 fixed), and the ExitStack/attached baggage context may also leak. Both tracing helpers are written best-effort (they swallow exceptions internally — bootstrap_tracing wraps everything, run_trace_context only acts after a successful import), so the probability is low, but the ordering is structurally fragile: any future change that lets these raise reintroduces an unended-run leak.
findings.suggested_fix: Move bootstrap_tracing() and trace_ctx.enter_context(run_trace_context(corr_id)) inside the existing try block (after the try:, before phase INGEST), so the finally's tracker.end_run cleanup covers a tracing-setup failure; or wrap them in their own try/except that records a tracking_error and proceeds untraced.

findings.title: scrubbed_env wipes all *_PROXY vars (fix #101), breaking the subscription call behind a corporate proxy
findings.description: Pass-1 fix #101 extends scrubbing to overwrite HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/ALL_PROXY/NO_PROXY (any-case '_PROXY' suffix), AWS_*, GOOGLE_*, and provider-redirect vars to empty string in the subprocess env. The high-stakes tier's Claude CLI subprocess makes an inherently REMOTE call to the Anthropic subscription endpoint (loopback-only is a DATA constraint per ADR-0003, not the LLM path). On an operator network that reaches the internet only via an egress proxy, wiping HTTPS_PROXY (and NO_PROXY) to '' makes the subscription auth/generation call fail outright — legitimate operation breaks. Confirmed: scrubbed_env() returns HTTPS_PROXY -> ''. This was a deliberate decision (PASS-1.md documents proxies as an exfiltration/SSRF channel for the full analysis prompt), so it is a designed tradeoff rather than an accidental defect — but it is an operability regression worth an explicit escape hatch.
findings.suggested_fix: If proxy stripping must stay for the threat model, document it prominently as a hard requirement (on-box direct egress to api.anthropic.com required) and/or provide an opt-in allowlist env (e.g. ASSAY_ALLOW_PROXY) that, when set, preserves a validated proxy var so proxied operators are not silently broken.

result.reasoning:
TRUE test-coverage gap, independently verified. The decision rule in confirm.py is inclusive at the threshold: verdict_from_pvalue uses `if p_value <= alpha` (line 130), and confirm_whole_corpus uses `if p_contra <= alpha` (line 284) and `if p_support <= alpha` (line 299). The docstrings document this inclusive '<= alpha' / 'beat the null at alpha' rule as intended behavior. The existing test test_verdict_mapping_covers_three_outcomes (lines 58-102) only exercises p=0.01 (clearly significant) and p=0.4 (clearly non-significant); the whole-corpus tests never produce an empirical p exactly equal to alpha. So the inclusive boundary (p == alpha) is never pinned by any assertion.

This is a test-quality defect, not a code defect: the production code is correct and matches its documentation. The risk is that a future regression flipping the documented inclusive rule to exclusive at the exact threshold would pass CI undetected. Severity LOW is correct (and I confirm the assessor's calibration): the production behavior is right today, and the boundary is probabilistically narrow — continuous p-values rarely land exactly on alpha, and the +1-smoothed empirical p only hits alpha for specific n/alpha pairs. Not covered by any hard-constraint exemption (local-first, no-API, ADR-0002/0006 are irrelevant here). The suggested fix (add p==alpha asserting SUPPORTED, and a whole-corpus case whose empirical p lands exactly on alpha) is the right minimal lock.
result.evidence:
Discriminating PoC (run against /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv): I flipped all three boundaries in /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py from `<= alpha` to `< alpha` — a real change to the decision rule for any statistic whose p-value/empirical-p equals alpha exactly (such inputs would now be ruled NOT significant). Results:
- `p_value <= alpha` -> `p_value < alpha` (line 130): `pytest tests/test_confirm.py` = 31 passed.
- `p_contra`/`p_support` `<= alpha` -> `< alpha` (lines 284, 299): full suite `pytest -q` = 407 passed, 5 skipped.
All green with the mutated decision rule, proving no test guards the p == alpha boundary in either verdict_from_pvalue or confirm_whole_corpus (and, transitively, the preregistration suite per the assessor's claim). Restored confirm.py afterward; `git diff` shows only pre-existing unrelated changes (pipeline.py, reasoning/seam.py), and all three `<= alpha` lines are intact.

Code/docs confirming the inclusive rule is intended (so `<` is a true behavior change, not an equivalent refactor): confirm.py:106 ("significant and direction agrees with the claim -> supported"), confirm.py:130, and confirm_whole_corpus docstring confirm.py:232 ("beat the null at `alpha`").

Test gap location: /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/tests/test_confirm.py:58-102 (only p=0.01 and p=0.4 used).

result.reasoning:
CONFIRMED TRUE — independently reproduced. This is a genuine test-quality defect: the most security-critical wiring in the codebase (the no-metered-API credential firewall, ADR-0003) is not guarded by any test, so a regression would ship with green CI.

What I verified by reading the real code:
- seam.py:382-389: _high_stakes_complete constructs ClaudeAgentOptions(..., env=scrubbed_env()). This env kwarg is what prevents the claude-agent-sdk subprocess from inheriting ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL / proxies / AWS/GOOGLE creds. scrubbed_env() (seam.py:160-174) correctly OVERWRITES unsafe vars to "" (not omit) so the sdk's {**os.environ, **options.env} merge wins.
- test_reasoning_drain.py is the ONLY test exercising _high_stakes_complete. Its fake _ClaudeAgentOptions (line 53-55) stores all kwargs in __dict__, and its fake query() (line 63) receives `options` — but no test ever reads options.env. So the fixture has the data but asserts nothing about it.
- The isolated function scrubbed_env() IS unit-tested (test_reasoning.py:71-93, test_scrubbed_env_overwrites_unsafe_and_survives_sdk_merge) — but only the pure function, never its USE/wiring into the options.

PoC (mutation testing) — exactly as the assessor claimed:
1. Baseline: test_reasoning_drain.py = 12 passed; full reasoning suite = 78 passed, 1 skipped.
2. Deleted the entire line `env=scrubbed_env(),` from _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:388).
3. Re-ran: test_reasoning_drain.py = 12 passed (still green); full reasoning suite = 78 passed, 1 skipped (still green).

result.reasoning:
CONFIRMED, real production-readiness defect. Both StudyDefinition.__post_init__ (study.py:38-51) and DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ (firewalls.py:97-111) declare frozenset-typed fields on @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True) records but never coerce/validate them. The raw constructors — which are the public API surface a clone uses (and which existing tests test_contract.py:59,70 and test_pipeline.py:521 already exercise directly) — silently accept a plain set. PoC verified: StudyDefinition(..., modes={StudyMode.DISCOVERY}).modes is a set; hash(definition) raises TypeError: unhashable type 'set'; and definition.modes.add(...) mutates a 'frozen' record in place. Same for DiscoverConfirmSplit with a plain set.

This is internally consistent with — and a gap in — the codebase's OWN stated invariant. src/assay_engine/_frozen.py exists precisely to fix this exact bug class for frozen records: its docstring (citing audit pass 1 issue #7, pass 2 #19, pass 3 #26/#27) calls out that frozen=True auto-generates __hash__ over all fields so instances 'look hashable but raise TypeError the moment they are hashed,' and that frozen only blocks rebinding while the contained collection stays mutable in place. Every other affected dataclass normalizes its collection fields through freeze() in __post_init__. modes / discovery_ids / confirm_ids are the remaining frozen collection fields that escaped that normalization on the raw-constructor path. So this is a genuine miss, not a re-report of pass-1 #101-#122 nor a style nit, and aligns with the project's hardening standard.

Calibration: I keep MEDIUM (assessor's rating) but note it is at the LOW/MEDIUM boundary because there is no LIVE functional breakage today: I confirmed no engine code path Python-hashes these records or uses them as set/dict keys (registry.py keys on the name string; pipeline.py holds them as plain fields), and the pre-registration/canonical hash path (_canonical.py canonical_plain lines 50-51) treats set and frozenset identically, so pre-registration anchoring is unaffected by the wrong runtime type. The defect is therefore (a) a latent landmine that breaks the instant any future code hashes/dedups/set-keys a definition or split, and (b) a false public-API contract: a documented frozen+immutable+hashable value object that is in fact neither when built via its supported raw constructor. The suggested fix (object.__setattr__ frozenset coercion, ideally with element-type validation, in both __post_init__ methods) is correct and matches the pattern used elsewhere in the engine.

result.reasoning:
CONFIRMED — genuine docs-vs-code over-claim, independently verified against code and docs.

CODE FACT: The high-stakes reasoning tier provably sends prompt content off-box. `_high_stakes_complete` (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:361-439) lazily imports `claude_agent_sdk` and runs `query(prompt=prompt, options=options)` (line 399). That call spawns the `claude` CLI which contacts Anthropic's hosted frontier model over the network. The module docstring (seam.py:9-12) states HIGH_STAKES is "a frontier model via a fixed-cost subscription path (the claude CLI / Agent SDK using an OAuth token)" — there is NO local frontier model; the only local tier is BULK (Ollama, loopback). scrubbed_env() (seam.py:160-174) strips metered creds and proxies but the legitimate subscription OAuth call still reaches Anthropic. Pass-1 finding #1 (docs/hardening/PASS-1.md) itself states "The high-stakes prompt is the full analysis content" sent over the network — the hardening team only fixed redirect/metered leakage, never the inherent off-box nature of the legitimate path.

REACHABILITY: `StakesTier` (incl. HIGH_STAKES) is a publicly exported first-class API member (src/assay_engine/reasoning/__init__.py:17,31). A study's adapter callable can legitimately issue `ReasoningRequest(tier=StakesTier.HIGH_STAKES, prompt=...)` with a prompt derived from the analyzed data. Not dead code.

DOC FACTS: Normative docs make UNCONDITIONAL residency guarantees with no qualifier for the high-stakes tier:
- README L71: "Everything runs on-box (local-first, data-sovereign); nothing leaves the machine."
- CHARTER §2.5 (L32-33): "All computation, storage, and observability run on-box. No data leaves the machine."
- GOVERNANCE §5 (L124-127): "All computation, storage, tracing, and experiment tracking run on-box. No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine."
- ADR-0003 L16-17: "...fixed-cost subscription path (no metered API)... No data, embeddings, traces, or results leave the machine." and L23 "runnable fully offline; nothing depends on a remote service to advance."

A grep over docs/ and README found ZERO qualifying language exempting the high-stakes tier (the only "off-box" mentions are in the PASS-1 audit log and in clauses describing things being disqualified). The docs conflate "no metered API" (a billing model) with "on-box" (data residency) — these are independent. ADR-0003's own Context (L7) says the data "may be sensitive, licensed, or otherwise constrained," which is exactly the scenario where the over-claim is dangerous: an operator trusting the absolute guarantee for such data who routes through HIGH_STAKES will exfiltrate data-derived prompt content.

NOT a false positive: this is not a justified-scope exclusion. The hard constraints given to me ("local-first/data-sovereign (loopback only)," "no metered API (subscription OAuth)") describe the *intended billing/transport posture*, but they do NOT license docs to claim absolute residency. The constraint "no metered API" is satisfied; the claim "nothing leaves the machine" is not, because the subscription path is still a network call to Anthropic.

SEVERITY recalibration: pass-2 assessor said HIGH; I downgrade to MEDIUM. It is a real production-readiness defect (a normative over-claim that could mislead a compliance/data-governance reviewer or operator handling licensed/sensitive data), but it is a documentation-accuracy defect, not a code vulnerability — the off-box transmission only occurs if a study deliberately selects the HIGH_STAKES tier, and the operator can avoid it entirely (BULK tier or UnconfiguredReasoningSeam). The actual code-level exfiltration risk (env redirect to arbitrary endpoints) was already a separate HIGH finding fixed in Pass-1 #101. What remains here is the residual documentation honesty gap, which warrants MEDIUM: meaningful misrepresentation in governance-critical docs, bounded impact, easy fix (qualify the guarantee).

- claim_id 'c1' yielded 3x -> SourceScorecard total=3, n_supported=3, alignment_rate=1.0. A single claim inflated the decisive denominator 3x with no error.
- hypothesis_id 'H1' returned 2x by discover -> 2 verdicts emitted, no error.

WHY IT IS REAL AND NOT OUT-OF-SCOPE:
1. ADR-0008 point #2 explicitly elevates claim<->hypothesis<->verdict IDENTITY to a runner-enforced invariant whose stated purpose is that a bug "cannot silently misattribute a verdict and pollute the scorecard." The runner checks attribution correctness (source_claim_id==claim_id, verdict.hypothesis_id==hypothesis.hypothesis_id) but never checks that each identity occurs once — uniqueness is the missing half of the very invariant the ADR claims to own.
2. Uniqueness IS a first-class, enforced identity invariant everywhere else in the engine: Corpus raises ValueError on duplicate unit_id (schema.py:80) and FeatureMatrix rejects duplicate unit_ids (pipeline.py:328-332). Claims/hypotheses are the inconsistent exception, so this is an internal-consistency gap, not a documented residual.
3. It is NOT one of the documented out-of-scope residuals. The firewall docstrings explicitly carve out only (a) frame-reflection/willful defeat and (b) metadata smuggling. The duplicate-id case is the *accidental* corruption mode (an adapter fan-out join, a CSV with repeated rows) that the engine repeatedly commits to catching ("guarantee is against accidental circularity"). Duplicate claim_ids are nowhere named as accepted scope.
4. METHODOLOGY §5 / the SourceScorecard docstring define alignment_rate as the fraction of *decisive claims* — an identity-level frequency. Multiplicity-weighting the denominator silently violates that definition.

WHY MEDIUM, NOT HIGH (recalibrated down):
- Impact is bounded to the §5 source-scoring metric (a reported frequency), not to a firewall guarantee or to data integrity of the baseline. No Firewall A/B property is breached; pre-registration and blindness are untouched.
- It is a correctness/robustness gap surfaced by malformed-but-plausible adapter input, not an attacker-controlled or remotely-triggerable path (this is a local-first, data-sovereign engine — the adapter author is the operator). The "a source can bias the rate" framing overstates adversariality; the realistic trigger is an accidental adapter bug.
- It corrupts a derived score silently (no crash, no log), which is the harmful part and why it is a genuine production defect worth fixing, but it does not rise to HIGH given the bounded blast radius and operator-trust model.

SUGGESTED FIX (matches the assessor and the engine's existing pattern): in adjudicate_with_baseline track seen source_claim_id/claim_id and raise FirewallViolation on repeat; in discover_and_confirm and the pipeline confirm loop track seen hypothesis_id and raise on repeat — mirroring the duplicate-id guards already in Corpus/FeatureMatrix. The purpose of a resample-stability guard on a SUPPORTED verdict is to reject observed statistics that are fragile/non-reproducing flukes; measuring against the null center instead of the observed effect means a resample distribution that barely clears the null median (reproducing only the effect's SIGN, not its magnitude) earns stability=1.0 and passes the gate, even though it reproduces nothing of the observed effect.

Root cause matches the report exactly:

- _frozen.py:73 (freeze) deep-converts only isinstance(value, Mapping) and native sequences/sets; a duck-mapping falls through to the hash() check at line 96, which passes (objects are identity-hashable by default), so freeze() returns the live, mutable object unchanged.

This is a genuine production-readiness defect in a PUBLIC engine API (Measurement/fence are the documented integrity boundary that adapters/operators feed). That is an atypical shape; no in-tree producer constructs Measurements this way (Measurement is only defined in fence.py, constructed externally). So it is a latent contract violation on an uncommon input, not a default-path break — MEDIUM, matching the assessor's rating. The suggested structural fix (treat any object exposing keys()+__getitem__ or items() as a mapping in BOTH freeze() and _contains_interpretation) is safe: verified that array.array and str expose neither keys nor items (so array-like buffer handling at _frozen.py:85-94 is unaffected), and MappingProxyType is already an abc.Mapping.

Two contracts are defeated by the same input:
